summary:
The independence of Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania from the Soviet Union was achieved through a combination of mass nonviolent mobilization and skillful political maneuvering during the Soviet collapse. The Singing Revolution — so named for mass song festivals and protests that drew hundreds of thousands — galvanized national consciousness in all three republics. On August 23, 1989, the fiftieth anniversary of the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact that had sealed their annexation by the Soviet Union, approximately two million people formed a human chain stretching 675 kilometers through all three republics in a demonstration known as the Baltic Way.

All three Baltic states declared the restoration of their independence in 1990, but Soviet authorities refused recognition and attempted to reassert control through military force in January 1991, killing civilians in Vilnius and Riga. The [[event:3481|failed coup of August 1991]] against Gorbachev created the political opening for decisive action: within days, dozens of countries recognized Baltic independence, and the Soviet government itself recognized it on September 6, 1991. The Baltic states subsequently pursued rapid integration with Western institutions, joining NATO and the European Union in 2004, completing their reorientation away from Russia.
